answers.value:
I am nominating myself, Emma Cartwright, as a North Notts Food & Drink Hero because over the last seven years The Green Hearth Bistro has become much more than a restaurant – it has grown into a community hub, a training ground for young local talent, and a living experiment in ethical, seasonal, low‑waste cooking.

I was born and raised just outside Worksop and grew up on my grandparents’ smallholding. From an early age I understood what it means to eat with the seasons and to know the people behind your food. When I opened The Green Hearth Bistro in 2019, my aim was to bring that same sense of connection into the heart of town: simple, thoughtful food where we can name the farm, the mill, or the bee keeper behind almost every ingredient.

Around 80% of our produce is sourced from within a 30‑mile radius. Our vegetables come from Carr Farm Organics in Retford, our beef and lamb from the Wilson family in Bothamsall, and our flour from Tuxford Windmill. We pay our suppliers fairly and agree prices in advance so small producers can plan their planting and investment with confidence. During the energy and cost‑of‑living spikes in 2023–24, rather than pushing for discounts, I cut back our menu and reduced my own salary so we could continue paying local farmers on time and at the agreed price.

Being a food hero, to me, is also about responsibility. We have reduced our general waste by over 70% since opening. All our veg trimmings are turned into stocks, ferments, or staff meals; any remaining organic waste goes to a local community composting scheme. We eliminated single‑use plastic in the dining room (no sauce sachets, no plastic straws) and moved to refillable cleaning products from a Mansfield supplier. Our fish menu was completely rewritten after I completed the Marine Stewardship Council’s Chef Ambassador training; we now only serve species rated 1–3 on the Good Fish Guide and explain those choices to guests.

The Green Hearth has also become a place where young people in North Notts can get a start in hospitality. We currently have four apprentices in partnership with North Notts College. Since 2019, twelve apprentices have come through our kitchen; ten are still working in the local hospitality sector, and two now run their own micro‑bakeries at village markets. I personally mentor every apprentice, running weekly skills sessions that cover not just knife work and sauce making but also menu costing, allergen management, and mental health in a high‑pressure kitchen.

Community is at the heart of what we do. Every Wednesday afternoon we close for regular trade and host a “Community Table” – a pay‑what‑you‑can two‑course lunch aimed at older residents, carers, and anyone feeling isolated. We started with 6 guests in 2020; we now serve an average of 28 people each week, many of whom tell us it’s the only time they eat with others around a table. We also partner with a local youth charity to run a monthly “Cook & Chat” session for 14–18 year‑olds, teaching basic cooking skills using affordable ingredients and sending them home with recipe cards tailored to what’s in season.

During the pandemic, when we were forced to close, we converted the entire bistro into a meal production space and worked with Bassetlaw District Council and local churches to provide over 9,000 hot meals between April 2020 and March 2021 to shielding residents and key workers. I personally coordinated volunteers, wrote menus that could be produced safely with a skeleton team, and spent many nights delivering meals myself in my small hatchback when we were short on drivers.

Ultimately, I believe I qualify as a Food & Drink Hero not because everything we do is perfect, but because we use food as a vehicle for dignity, connection, and environmental responsibility. Every menu choice, every supplier conversation, and every community project is guided by the question: “Does this make North Notts a better place to live and eat?” I am proud of what we have built so far and would be honoured for The Green Hearth Bistro, and by extension our local producers and community, to be recognised through this award.

answers.value:
Qualifications & Training:
- Level 3 Professional Cookery (City & Guilds) – North Notts College, 2010.
- Level 3 Award in Supervising Food Safety in Catering, 2018; renewed Level 2 Food Safety for all staff annually.
- Level 3 Award in Leadership & Management (ILM), completed 2021 to strengthen my ability to run an ethical, people‑focused business.
- Marine Stewardship Council (MSC) Chef Ambassador online training, 2022.
- Completed the Sustainable Restaurant Association’s “Food Made Good” sustainability module, 2023.

Awards & Recognitions:
- "Best Newcomer" – North Notts Business Awards, 2020 (The Green Hearth Bistro).
- Finalist, "Sustainable Business of the Year" – North Notts Business Awards, 2022.
- Included in the Nottinghamshire Good Food Guide 2024 as one of the top 20 independent eateries in the county.
- Hygiene Rating: 5 (Very Good) from Bassetlaw District Council, consistently maintained since first inspection in 2019 (latest inspection: 2025-03-14).

Press & Media:
- Featured in the Worksop Guardian (2021-11-05): "Local Bistro Turns Surplus Veg into Community Feasts" – article highlighting our low‑waste approach and Community Table lunches.
- Interviewed on BBC Radio Nottingham (2022-06-18) about our apprenticeship scheme and how we support young people into hospitality careers.
- Profile piece in the North Notts Food & Drink Trail brochure 2023, focusing on our use of local producers and our zero‑plastic front‑of‑house policy.

Community & Charity Work:
- Co‑founded the "North Notts Community Kitchen Network" in 2021, bringing together five local cafés and pubs to coordinate surplus food donations to shelters and food banks.
- Regular in‑kind supporter of the Bassetlaw Food Bank: we provide a monthly delivery of homemade frozen soups and stews using surplus vegetables.
- Host venue for the annual "Big Lunch" community event on the first Sunday in June, offering our space and staff free of charge; in 2024 we served 120 people using ingredients donated by local producers.

Social Media & Online Evidence:
- Instagram highlights show behind‑the‑scenes stories of farm visits to Carr Farm Organics and Tuxford Windmill; posts dated 2024-04-12 and 2024-09-03 demonstrate our local sourcing in practice.
- Facebook album "Community Table Stories" (launched 2022) contains photos and anonymised testimonials from regular attendees, including handwritten notes from older guests about the difference these lunches have made to their social lives.
- TripAdvisor reviews frequently mention our staff’s knowledge of allergens and our flexibility in creating gluten‑free, dairy‑free, and vegetarian options without compromising on quality. As of January 2026 we hold a 4.8/5 rating from over 320 reviews.

Additional Notes:
- All front‑of‑house and kitchen staff are paid at or above the Real Living Wage. We publish our pay structure internally and involve senior staff in financial planning meetings so the whole team understands how ethical decisions (e.g. paying farmers fairly, reducing waste) affect our margins.
- We track our food waste by weight weekly. In 2020 we averaged 42kg/week of food waste; by the end of 2025 this had reduced to 11kg/week, despite serving more covers, due to improved menu planning, portion control based on customer feedback, and creative use of trimmings.
- We have recently started collaborating with a local secondary school to design a 6‑week "Food, Place & Planet" module, where Year 9 students visit the bistro, meet our suppliers, and cook a simple seasonal menu. The pilot ran in autumn 2025 with 18 students; we have been asked to repeat and expand it in 2026.

These details, alongside the main narrative, demonstrate a consistent, long‑term commitment to ethical sourcing, community engagement, staff development, and environmental responsibility – all rooted firmly in North Nottinghamshire.
